title: The Alliterative Morte Arthure
summary: The repository artifact is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item AllitMA. U-M's header says the electronic text was made available through the Oxford Text Archive and identifies Valerie Krishna's 1976 critical edition as its printed source. Krishna's edition represents the poem's sole surviving witness, Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 (the Lincoln Thornton manuscript), fols. 53r–98v. For visual comparison, U-M's encoding declaration cites the 1975 Scolar Press facsimile of the complete Thornton manuscript. A modern METS edition is linked as a separate comparison source, not as an upstream source of this repository file.
review_notes: The exact technical handoff between the OTA SGML-tagged version and U-M's structurally enriched XML has not been documented; the relation is therefore supported rather than asserted as byte-for-byte copying.

Current provider statements, an embedded historical availability notice, and copyright in the 1976 editorial layer are recorded separately. Access to a digital text is not treated as permission to redistribute manuscript photographs.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for AllitMA
entities.description: The checked-in XML source used by this repository for the work.
notes: The path name CME_from_OTA is consistent with the XML header's OTA provenance, but path naming alone is not used to infer the entire transformation history.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AllitMA
entities.description: U-M Humanities Text Initiative/CME structured digital text, published in 1993 and exposing 4,346 numbered lines.

entities.label: Oxford Text Archive item ota:1675
entities.description: OTA SGML-tagged encoding from Krishna's edition, described by the current record as an SGML-tagged version of OTA text 535. The record lists allitma-1675.txt, but anonymous bitstream download was not available when checked.
notes: The record exposes 1440 in a created/date field, but that value reflects the medieval work or witness era rather than the separately stated 1992 digital issue date.
The .txt filename does not establish that the file is untagged plain text; the official record expressly calls this an SGML-tagged version.

entities.label: Valerie Krishna's 1976 critical edition
alternate_labels: The Alliterative Morte Arthure: A Critical Edition
entities.description: The immediate printed source named by both the repository XML and OTA record.

bibliographic.citation: The Alliterative Morte Arthure: A Critical Edition, edited with introduction, notes, and glossary by Valerie Krishna (New York: Burt Franklin, 1976).
bibliographic.title: The Alliterative Morte Arthure: A Critical Edition

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral MS 91
entities.description: The sole known manuscript witness of the poem; Morte Arthure occupies fols. 53r–98v and is written in long lines by Robert Thornton.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.notes: This dates the manuscript, not necessarily the poem's composition.

physical_description.format: Codex; the poem is written in long lines
notes: The catalogue describes the manuscript as written throughout by one scribe, Robert Thornton, and associated with East Newton, Yorkshire.

entities.label: The Thornton Manuscript (Lincoln Cathedral MS 91), Scolar Press facsimile
entities.description: Printed facsimile edition of the full Thornton manuscript with introductions by D. S. Brewer and A. E. B. Owen; explicitly cited in U-M's encoding declaration.

bibliographic.citation: The Thornton Manuscript (Lincoln Cathedral MS 91), introductions by D. S. Brewer and A. E. B. Owen (London: Scolar Press, 1975).
bibliographic.title: The Thornton Manuscript (Lincoln Cathedral MS 91)

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral online catalogue record for MS 91
entities.description: The holding institution's detailed contents and physical description for the Thornton manuscript.

entities.label: Benson–Foster METS edition of the Alliterative Morte Arthure
entities.description: A freely readable modern scholarly edition linked for textual comparison; it is not an upstream source of AllitMA.xml.

bibliographic.citation: Larry D. Benson, ed., revised by Edward E. Foster, King Arthur's Death: The Middle English Stanzaic Morte Arthur and Alliterative Morte Arthure (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1994).

agents.name: University of Michigan Library
alternate_names: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative

agents.name: Robert Thornton
notes: Scribe and compiler of the manuscript witness, not the poem's identified author.

subject.description: The complete local AllitMA XML artifact

scope.notes: The local file carries U-M's title, DLPS identifier, 1993 publication statement, and content structure; the precise date and mechanics of repository ingestion are not recorded.

The U-M header says the text was made available through OTA, and the two resources represent the same Krishna-derived text in different structures. The available evidence does not establish transfer direction or which institution introduced each structural feature.

subject.description: OTA SGML-tagged version of the complete poem

object.description: The edited poem in Krishna's critical edition
Morte Arthure in the sole surviving witness

scope.notes: The source is a critical edition, not a photographic or strictly diplomatic facsimile.

object.description: The Morte Arthure leaves and their visible sectioning

scope.notes: U-M's encoding declaration cites the facsimile immediately after explaining that manuscript structure sometimes overrides Krishna's paragraph divisions.

subject.description: The Alliterative Morte Arthure portion of the METS edition

scope.notes: This is a related modern edition for comparison, not an upstream source of AllitMA.xml.

access.access_method: Read the checked-out CME submodule path.
Read the item online; the table of contents exposes the complete text.
The public OTA record lists item files, but direct anonymous bitstream requests are disallowed and the download-all route redirects to OTA login.
notes: A successful authenticated download was not verified; registration may not by itself guarantee access.

access.access_method: Open Library links to an Internet Archive controlled digital lending copy marked Preview/Borrow; availability may vary by account and lending state.
access.format: Controlled-borrow scan or limited Google Books view
notes: This is access, not an authorization to redistribute the scan or the edition's protected editorial matter.

access.access_method: Read the holding institution's catalogue description online.
access.format: HTML catalog record with one repository-photographic-library thumbnail

access.access_method: Apply in writing with the subject and purpose of research, provide an academic letter of introduction, and book the reading room in advance; confirm item availability during library works.
notes: The public page says all library resources, including medieval manuscripts, are available for study, but also reports temporary collection-access limitations during Wren Library repairs. Availability should be confirmed before travel.
The academic-letter requirement may make access difficult for independent researchers; contact the librarian to ask what alternative evidence of a serious research purpose is acceptable.

access.access_method: Request digital images or photocopies from the Cathedral librarian; charges apply and item-specific availability and reuse terms must be confirmed.
access.format: Digital images or photocopies on request
notes: No complete public image set or current price schedule for MS 91 was found. Ask for a quote and written reuse terms before ordering.

access.access_method: Consult the printed facsimile in the Wellcome Collection Library; the catalog lists it on open shelves.
Read the Middle English edition online or use the site's download link subject to METS terms.
access.format: HTML scholarly edition with glosses and notes

rights.component: The AllitMA materials presented through U-M's CME item
rights.basis: U-M expressly labels the materials public domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
notes: No Copyright–United States is a provider rights statement, not a worldwide license and not a statement about newly supplied manuscript photography.
The provider assertion is recorded as stated; it does not independently resolve the protectability of every contribution in Krishna's 1976 edition.

rights.component: The OTA item files described by the current repository record
rights.basis: The current OTA item page applies Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported to the record even though anonymous file download was not available when checked.
notes: The license assertion and practical file access are separate facts. Do not assume it licenses independent scans of Krishna's book or Lincoln Cathedral MS 91.

rights.component: The checked-in XML transcription and structure derived from the U-M/OTA lineage
rights.jurisdiction: United States and applicable provider terms
rights.basis: U-M currently asserts No Copyright–United States and OTA currently offers its file under CC BY 3.0, but the XML embeds an older 'personal scholarly use only' OTA notice and ultimately transcribes a 1976 critical edition whose protectable editorial contributions may remain copyrighted.
notes: This deliberately conservative status records unresolved scope and historical notice conflicts; it is not legal advice.
Repository-authored code, metadata, typesetting, and generated outputs can have separate licenses.

rights.component: Original introduction, critical apparatus, notes, glossary, selection and arrangement, and any sufficiently original editorial readings
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1976. U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A states that qualifying works copyrighted in 1964–1977 renew automatically and can receive a 95-year term; absent edition-specific notice and ownership research, protectable 1976 contributions should not be presumed public domain merely because the medieval text is old.
notes: If federal copyright was validly secured, a 95-year term measured from 1976 would run through 2071. This is conditional, not a finding that every individual reading or transcription choice is protected.
Open Library/Internet Archive borrowing does not authorize redistribution.

rights.component: The anonymous medieval textual content embodied in the manuscript
rights.basis: The text and its sole surviving fifteenth-century witness are centuries beyond any U.S. copyright term.
notes: This concerns the medieval text, not modern photography, conservation images, catalog prose, or conditions attached to supplied files.

rights.component: Any digital images or photocopies supplied by Lincoln Cathedral in response to a request
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The public reader page says digital images and photocopies are available for a charge but does not supply item-specific copyright, publication, or repository-reuse terms.
notes: Obtain written permission or terms before publishing supplied files in a repository or print-on-demand workflow.

rights.component: The 1975 facsimile's photographic images, introductions, layout, and other modern contributions
rights.basis: The facsimile was published in 1975 with modern introductions and photographic reproduction; no open license for the volume or its page images was found.
notes: It is a useful visual comparison source, but a library holding does not authorize republication of its images.

rights.component: Modern edition, introduction, glosses, notes, translation or normalization, and site presentation
rights.basis: This is a modern scholarly edition presented by METS; public web access is not treated as public-domain status or a blanket republication grant.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Paragraph and section divisions in Krishna's edition compared with visible manuscript structure
editorial_practices.representation: Krishna's paragraph divisions are generally followed, but U-M adds structural divisions where manuscript sections do not coincide.
editorial_practices.description: The encoding declaration says manuscript structural integrity overrides Krishna's paragraph divisions in a few cases and cites the 1975 Thornton facsimile.
notes: The XML's 80 DIV1 ranges therefore combine printed-edition organization with manuscript-aware intervention and must not be described as authorial chapters.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Illustrations in the printed source edition
editorial_practices.representation: Not encoded or noted in the electronic text
editorial_practices.description: The source description expressly states that illustrations in the printed edition were not noted in the electronic text.

evidence.summary: The file identifies U-M HTI, OTA U-1675-A, Krishna's 1976 critical edition, Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, and the 1975 Thornton facsimile; it also records the manuscript-aware paragraph policy and omission of printed illustrations.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, The alliterative Morte Arthure, item information.
evidence.summary: The item identifies the anonymous title, Burt Franklin c. 1976 publication, OTA U-1675-A, a complete 4,346-line text, and U-M's public-domain/No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive, Alliterative Morte Arthure, ota:1675.
evidence.locator: Full item record, file list, access controls, abstract, and rights label
evidence.summary: The record identifies a 1992-04-03 issue date, describes ota:1675 as an SGML-tagged version of text 535 transcribed from Valerie Krishna's edition, lists allitma-1675.txt, and applies CC BY 3.0. The displayed bitstreams currently disallow anonymous access, direct file requests return to the item page, and download-all redirects to login.

evidence.citation: Google Books bibliographic record, Valerie Krishna, The Alliterative Morte Arthure: A Critical Edition.
evidence.summary: The record identifies Valerie Krishna as editor, Burt Franklin as publisher, 1976 as publication year, ISBN 089102039X, and 361 pages.

evidence.citation: Open Library record OL4895339M, The alliterative Morte Arthure: a critical edition.
evidence.summary: The record supplies ISBN, LCCN, OCLC, pagination, and a Preview/Borrow link to Internet Archive controlled digital lending.

evidence.citation: Lincoln Cathedral Library, MS 91, The 'Thornton' Romances.
evidence.locator: Record MS 91, item 4 and physical description
evidence.summary: The official record places Morte Arthure at fols. 53–98v, quotes its beginning and explicit, identifies Robert Thornton as scribe, dates the manuscript c. 1430–1450, and describes 322 paper leaves measuring 290 × 210 mm.

evidence.locator: How to become a reader; reading-room rules; digital images and photocopies
evidence.summary: The page describes application and appointment requirements, gives the librarian's contact, says manuscript study is available subject to current access limitations, and states that digital images and photocopies may be requested for a charge.

evidence.citation: Lincoln Cathedral online catalogue, About the collections.
evidence.summary: The catalogue explains that manuscripts are available for academic research by prior appointment and that photographic images and photocopies incur charges.

evidence.citation: Wellcome Collection, The Thornton manuscript (Lincoln Cathedral MS. 91), 1975.
evidence.locator: About this work and where to find it
evidence.summary: The record identifies the 1975 Scolar Press volume as a facsimile edition with introductions by D. S. Brewer and A. E. B. Owen, approximately 700 pages, and lists an open-shelf Wellcome holding.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, King Arthur's Death: The Middle English Stanzaic Morte Arthur and Alliterative Morte Arthure.
evidence.locator: Edition description, credits, publication data, and text links
evidence.summary: The page identifies Larry D. Benson and Edward E. Foster, the 1994 print publication, and the online Alliterative Morte Arthure text.

evidence.citation: Larry D. Benson, Introduction to King Arthur's Death, revised by Edward E. Foster, Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.summary: The introduction discusses the poem's probable date and region, form, relation to chronicle history and Malory, and cites Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 and Krishna's 1976 edition.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Alliterative Morte Arthure.
evidence.summary: The database identifies Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, fols. 53r–98v, as the sole witness and lists Krishna's 1976 critical edition.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright, revised April 2026.
evidence.locator: Works already under statutory protection before 1978; automatic renewal for works copyrighted 1964–1977
evidence.summary: The Copyright Office explains that qualifying works copyrighted from 1964 through 1977 renew automatically and may receive a total 95-year term, while all U.S. works published before 1931 are public domain as of 2026.

open_questions.question: What exact conversion, correction, or structural-enrichment steps produced U-M's XML from OTA item 1675?
next_steps: Obtain authorized access to the OTA SGML-tagged file, then compare it with U-M XML character by character while separately comparing their headers and structure.
Ask U-M Digital Collections whether process documentation or an original deposit package survives.

open_questions.question: Does Lincoln Cathedral have a complete digital facsimile of MS 91 that can be supplied or licensed for repository reference?
next_steps: Email the Cathedral librarian with shelfmark MS 91 and folios 53r–98v, requesting availability, quote, resolution, delivery format, and written reuse terms.
Ask whether the one catalog thumbnail belongs to a larger internal image set.
Use the 1975 printed facsimile onsite for comparison if a digital order is unavailable or prohibitive.

open_questions.question: What copyrights were secured in Krishna's 1976 edition, and what scope did OTA and U-M have authority to license or label?
next_steps: Inspect the 1976 volume's copyright page and publication notice through controlled borrowing or a holding library.
Search U.S. Copyright Office public records for the edition and relevant renewal/ownership data.
Ask OTA and U-M whether their current labels cover the transcription file alone or all editorial readings reproduced from the source edition.

open_questions.question: How should the XML's historical 'personal scholarly use only' OTA notice be reconciled with OTA's current CC BY 3.0 label and U-M's current No Copyright–United States statement?
next_steps: Preserve all dated statements in provenance records rather than deleting the historical notice.
Request clarification from OTA and U-M before relying on the current labels for commercial redistribution of the transcription.
